ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Chongqing Laziji; closest defensible match is fried chicken food product (FOODON_03315763). The dish involves deep-frying followed by stir-frying with dried chilies and Sichuan peppercorns.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Chongqing-style spicy fried chicken. A0CKP (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. The deep-frying and stir-frying preparation with dried chilies and Sichuan peppercorns is not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chongqing Spicy Chicken or equivalent Sichuan fried chicken dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One sharing plate of Chongqing Spicy Chicken weighs approximately 350 g and is typically shared between two to three diners as part of a multi-dish Chinese meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (SR Legacy 05062) adapted with chili and seasoning adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chongqing Laziji exists in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry 05062 (fried battered chicken, meat and skin) as the primary anchor for the deep-fried chicken component, cross-referenced with USDA entries for dried chili peppers (11819) and garlic (11215) to account for the chili-heavy stir-fry component. The input record values (energy 242.86 kcal, protein 15.71 g, fat 17.14 g, carbohydrate 5.71 g per 100 g) are consistent with this adapted profile and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 514.29 mg/100 g reflects soy sauce and salt seasoning typical of this dish. All values are per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate based on fried chicken with low-carbohydrate coating.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chongqing Laziji in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on analogous fried chicken with minimal starch coating, consistent with the low carbohydrate content of 5.71 g/100 g. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (45 × 20.0) / 100 = 9.0 — however, accounting for the chili and non-digestible fibre fraction reducing available carbohydrate to approximately 4.57 g/100 g × 3.5 serving = 16.0 g available carbohydrate per serving: GL = (45 × 8.0) / 100 = 3.6. This is a conservative low-end estimate. Clinicians should treat this as an approximation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 514 mg per 100 g; a single 350 g sharing portion contributes approximately 1,800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100–150 g per sitting, request reduced soy sauce preparation, and avoid adding additional salt or condiments at the table to keep total meal sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5.71 g/100 g) and high protein (15.71 g/100 g) moderate postprandial glucose response, but deep-frying increases caloric density and fat load, which can impair long-term insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a moderate portion (100–150 g) of this dish provided it is paired with non-starchy vegetables rather than white rice, and total meal energy is monitored to avoid caloric excess that worsens insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (17.14 g/100 g), elevated sodium (514 mg/100 g), and deep-frying preparation increase cardiovascular risk through atherogenic lipid load, blood pressure elevation, and pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its high sodium and fat content from deep-frying; if consumed, a maximum portion of 100 g with no added salt and paired with potassium-rich vegetables is advised to partially offset sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation per EULAR dietary guidelines.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations of this dish; a stir-fried-only version without deep-frying would substantially reduce AGE load, and the anti-inflammatory aromatics (ginger, garlic) provide partial benefit that cannot offset the AGE burden of the standard preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chongqing Spicy Chicken (重庆辣子鸡, Chóngqìng Làzi Jī) is a bold and intensely flavoured Sichuan-Chinese dish originating from Chongqing municipality in southwestern China. The dish is prepared by first marinating bite-sized bone-in chicken pieces in soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, and cornstarch, then deep-frying them until golden and crispy. The fried chicken is subsequently stir-fried in a wok with an extraordinarily generous quantity of dried red chilies — often outnumbering the chicken pieces visually — along with Sichuan peppercorns, garlic, ginger, and scallions. The defining sensory characteristic is the mala (麻辣) flavour profile: a simultaneous numbing sensation from Sichuan peppercorns (hydroxy-alpha-sanshool) and intense heat from the dried chilies, layered over the savoury, slightly smoky depth of the fried chicken. The texture contrasts crispy chicken skin with the chewy, aromatic dried chilies. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 243 kcal, 15.7 g protein, 17.1 g fat (3.4 g saturated), and 5.7 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 514 mg reflecting soy sauce and salt seasoning. The deep-frying preparation increases caloric density and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs). Clinically, the high sodium content is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management, while the low carbohydrate content and high protein make postprandial glycaemic impact moderate for Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled. The dish is widely served in Chinese restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia as a shared main course.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chongqing Laziji exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken data (entry 05062) and are consistent with the input record values. Medium confidence reflects the use of an analogous-source adaptation rather than a direct match. GI is an analogous estimate. Ontology codes are approximate matches only.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific Chongqing-style deep-fried and stir-fried chicken preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, Singapore HPB Foodo, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of April 2026. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.06 g/100 g from the input record, consistent with trace trans-fat from deep-frying oil; this is a plausible low value and is retained. All core macros (protein_g 15.71, fat_g 17.14, carbohydrate_g 5.71) are non-zero and consistent with the deep-fried chicken profile. No zero-macro justification is required. Halal status is marked false as standard preparation may use Shaoxing rice wine (alcohol-containing); halal-certified versions exist but cannot be assumed. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as supporting only menu_listing and has been superseded by USDA FoodData Central as the nutrient evidence anchor. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Chongqing Laziji was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026.
